title: Payment Failure & Involuntary Churn
business_definition: Payment failure rate = failed charges / total charge attempts. Failed payments cause involuntary churn — customers who want to stay but can't pay. Recovery via dunning (retry logic + notifications) is critical.
diagnostic_questions: What failure codes are most common — insufficient funds, expired card, bank decline?
Is the failure rate concentrated in a specific payment method or gateway?
What is the recovery rate after retry — is dunning logic working?
Did a processor or gateway change coincide with the failure spike?

causal_relationships.detection_sql: SELECT failure_code, payment_method, COUNT(*) AS failures, COUNT(*) * 100.0 / SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER () AS pct FROM payment_attempts WHERE status = 'failed' AND attempted_at >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days' GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 3 DESC

SELECT retry_number, COUNT(*) AS attempts, COUNT(CASE WHEN status = 'success' THEN 1 END) AS recovered, COUNT(CASE WHEN status = 'success' THEN 1 END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0) AS recovery_rate FROM payment_retries GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1

inflation_causes.cause: Duplicate retry attempts counted as separate failures
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT subscription_id, COUNT(*) AS attempts, MIN(attempted_at) AS first_attempt FROM payment_attempts WHERE status = 'failed' GROUP BY 1 HAVING COUNT(*) > 1

deflation_causes.cause: Soft declines (retry-able) not tracked separately from hard declines
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT decline_type, COUNT(*), AVG(CASE WHEN recovered_at IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS recovery_rate FROM payment_failures GROUP BY 1

anti_patterns: Do not treat all failed payment codes equally — expired card is recoverable, fraud block is not
Do not retry too aggressively — excessive retries increase bank flagging and worsen recovery rates

sql_assets.failure_rate_trend: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('week', attempted_at) AS week, COUNT(CASE WHEN status = 'failed' THEN 1 END) * 100.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0) AS failure_rate FROM payment_attempts GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1
